dimensions.note: First-publish full review. All credentials come from the environment: the ambient chain by default, fromIni for a named profile, and fromTemporaryCredentials (auto-refreshing AssumeRole) for org member accounts; nothing is vaulted or stored. The push-time credentials-sensitive-field warning on namePrefix is a scanner false positive — the default value is a CloudFormation stack-name prefix containing the word Password, not a secret.

Every method logs entry and completion at info with structured placeholders (never interpolation); per-stack and per-account progress logs cover the fan-out paths. No secrets appear in any log call — logged values are account ids, regions, stack names, statuses, and counters, all operationally expected for a fleet tool.

expectAccount mismatch throws before any writeResource. Org drivers implement the documented fatal-vs-skip split: discovery failures (GetCallerIdentity/ListAccounts) propagate, per-account failures are caught, recorded in org-summary.failures, and skipped. Catches are narrow: ValidationError does-not-exist for DescribeStacks, ResourceNotFoundException for DeleteFunction, NoSuchEntity for GetRole; everything else rethrows. Minor nit (non-blocking): a pass-2 retain refusal under apply is tallied in the errors counter while the same refusal pre-apply lands in skipped — the per-stack deletion row is honest in both cases (action skip with the reason).

22 pure-logic unit tests (guards, retain computation, classification, keys, slugs) plus 22 smoke tests driving runEnumerate/runEnumerateOrg/runCleanup/runCleanupOrg through injected fake SweepApi/OrgApi fixtures and a recording context: dry-run vs apply, wrong-account landing check, skip-dont-abort, no-self-assume, onlyAccount canary, counter aggregation, and network-budget guards. Colocated tests pin manifest-name/version sync, the method/resource surface, and the upgrade-chain invariant.

cleanup is re-runnable: already-gone stacks are recorded as gone and skipped, wait-budget exhaustion records delete-initiated/delete-retain and a later run resumes from live state (targets are re-enumerated live each run, never from stale rows). Org drivers never abort on a member failure. Partial interruption leaves per-stack deletion rows describing exactly what was initiated.

ListStacks, ListStackResources, and ListAccounts all drain NextToken pagination to completion (no caps, so no truncated flag is needed). Clients use maxAttempts 6 retries; optional SDK response fields are defaulted before use. Organizations is pinned to its us-east-1 global endpoint and the AssumeRole provider pins an explicit STS region so runs work without AWS_REGION set.

SDK clients are memoized per region in per-run maps and scoped to the run; no file handles or temp files. The abort signal is threaded through every SDK call and the poll delay (clearTimeout on abort), so cancellation does not leak timers.

All fixture account ids are synthetic repeated-digit placeholders (111111111111 / 222222222222 / 333333333333 / 999999999999), stack ids are arn:stack/<suffix> stubs, and no hostnames, IPs, or real topology appear in README, tests, or source. README was brought in sync with the shipped surface for this publish: it now documents all four methods including cleanupOrg and no longer lists cross-account cleanup as out of scope. Manifest version and model version are aligned at 2026.07.03.1.

Mechanical schema-write conformance verified for all four specs: every schema field is written by its writers, no data field is absent from a schema, no placeholder values, and the cleanup-only org-summary counters are declared optional and populated on the cleanupOrg path. Known deliberate deviation, disclosed: all four schemas use .passthrough() for forward-compat; the rationale is documented for org-summary (README and its schema comment — two writers share org-summary and add fields non-breakingly) while the orphan/summary/deletion passthroughs carry no comment, matching the repo precedent in aws-s3-bucket-audit; the tradeoff is weaker CEL validation of undeclared fields.
Every spec sets lifetime infinite with garbageCollection 10, matching the accumulate-and-query design where rows are keyed by account/region/stack and the last 10 versions per row are retained.

Not a CRUD lifecycle model: it inventories and retires pre-existing orphan stacks (enumerate/enumerateOrg/cleanup/cleanupOrg) and never creates or updates the underlying resources, so create/update/sync methods do not apply.

No labeled check definitions, but the mutating paths enforce equivalent pre-flight guards before any write or delete: apply defaults to false (dry-run plan rows only), expectAccount throws before any write, cleanupOrg forces a per-member expectAccount landing check, the namePrefix guard refuses out-of-scope stacks, and computeRetain refuses to retain anything but the detected custom resource.

Mechanical check done: orphan-/summary- rows are written by runEnumerate (reused by enumerateOrg), deletion- rows by runCleanup (reused by cleanupOrg), and org-summary-<mgmt> by both org drivers with the same schema shape, distinguished by the mode field. Keys embed account, region, and a sanitized stack segment, so writers cannot collide across accounts or specs.

Methods never read back stored rows (no dataRepository access): cleanup deliberately re-enumerates targets live rather than trusting a stale inventory, which is the safer design for a deleting method.

Initial publish at 2026.07.03.1 with a single no-op upgrades entry whose toVersion equals model.version; a colocated test pins the chain-ends-at-model.version invariant and that every entry passes attributes through unchanged.
